content: Finally, he stated that no concrete information has been received by the council about misuse of budget under the NGO/INGO software heading during the Gen Z movement of Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082.
21. Statement of Prison Administrator (Jailer), Under Secretary Satyaraj Joshi, of the Prison Office Nakkhu, Lalitpur:

On Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082, I was regularly present at my office. On Bhadra 23, the situation was normal. On Bhadra 24, from early morning, provocative materials began appearing on social media and unauthorized surveillance around the prison began increasing, after which I informed the Chief District Officer, Ministry of Home Affairs, the Peace and Security Division, and the Department of Prison Management, and called an emergency security meeting. At that meeting, discussions were held on crowd control strategy, alternative evacuation plans, and overall security preparedness.

At approximately 1:40 PM, thousands of demonstrators chanting 'Release Rabi Lamichhane' began attacking the main gate of the prison with stone-pelting and vandalism. Seeing that the crowd situation was going out of control, I again made written and oral requests to the Chief District Officer and Ministry of Home Affairs to deploy the army, but additional security force could not be made available immediately. At around 1:50 PM, the crowd broke through the main gate, forcibly entered, and took the prison under its control.

At that time, there were 1,281 prisoners and detainees in the prison. After the crowd began attacking targeting Rabi Lamichhane, keeping the security of other prisoners in mind, I made a decision to temporarily transfer him to the psychosocial disabled prisoner hospital inside the prison. Later, he asked for a microphone saying 'I will try to convince the crowd.' Thereafter, security personnel sent him before the crowd, but the crowd took him out of the prison. I immediately informed the Ministry of Home Affairs and the Chief District Officer about that incident.

After he was taken outside, the crowd became even more agitated. The prison administration records room and police building were set on fire, and both police and armed police were forced to retreat. I and administrative staff were hiding inside the psychosocial disabled prisoner hospital building trying to save our lives. In the course of that, the crowd subjected me to extreme threats and terrorized me with fear of life and death, forcing me to sign an unknown letter. I had no prior knowledge of the contents of the letter. Since that signature was made under coercion and in a terrorizing situation, it has no legal validity whatsoever.

Thereafter, the crowd set fire to and vandalized prisoner blocks and released all 1,280 prisoners and detainees outside. A few days after the incident, 749 prisoners and detainees returned to prison, 70 were transferred to other prisons, and 459 are still at large. On that day, in terms of human casualties, only some police officers sustained minor injuries.

My assessment is that the prison was saved from a major massacre precisely because the security personnel working at the prison demonstrated high restraint, wisdom, and leadership. In my view, the events of Bhadra 24 were not administrative negligence but were an inevitable circumstance beyond control.
For long-term improvement of prison security, I have presented the following recommendations:

content.content: Immediately implement a Contingency Plan and Emergency Response Protocol.
Construct a High-Security Zone or Isolated Unit for sensitive detainees.
Establish a Real-time Coordination System among Nepal Police, Armed Police Force, Prison Administration, and Local Administration.
Improve the prison's physical structure, surveillance system, and emergency exit routes, and provide crisis management training to staff.
Update the Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) for prison administration and implement it in all prisons.

content: 22. Account of Assistant Chief District Officer Muktiram Rijal, District Administration Office Kathmandu:

I recall that on Bhadra 22, 2082, Sudan Gurung, Purushottam Yadav, and Ankit Malla submitted applications at the District Administration Office Kathmandu requesting permission to hold a peaceful demonstration against the Government of Nepal's ban on certain social media platforms and related matters, and the applicants who came with these applications were informed by the office to demonstrate peacefully and that certain sensitive locations in Kathmandu had been designated as prohibited zones.

After receiving the said applications, a District Security Committee meeting was held on 2082/5/22 with detailed discussions on making the demonstration peaceful and protecting demonstrators and public property, and a decision was made for all security agencies to coordinately arrange flawless security to ensure the demonstration remained peaceful, and the 2082 Integrated Special Security Plan was also approved.

As directed by the Chief District Officer to report to the office at 7 AM the following day  Bhadra 23  I arrived at the office at 7 AM, a District Security Committee meeting was held reviewing the situation, and a decision was made to exercise full caution when using force for crowd control since students and children were expected to have significant participation in the demonstration.

Thereafter, District Security Committee officials remained at the District Administration Office and a Command Post was also established, and in the command center, another District Security Committee meeting decided to strengthen security management and to strengthen coordination among security agencies to prevent any kind of undesirable activity and arrange flawless security.

From around 10 AM, demonstrators began gathering in the Maitighar Mandala area. Although those demonstrators initially gathered in the Maitighar Mandala area, when numbers grew I saw all demonstrators advancing toward Baneswhar on the CCTV display connected to the Command Post. Since the daily office services were also continuing on that day, I was also engaged in secretarial work in addition to being a member of the District Security Committee and was not able to be at the Command Post to observe all scenes.

I saw on the CCTV display that the crowd advancing toward Baneswhar broke through the barricade placed in front of Everest Hotel at around 11 AM and was moving forward, and since the District Chief was present at the Command Post from school/college-age young women at the front of the crowd, I also heard directives being given to security officials at the Command Post to exercise restraint.

As demonstrators who had broken through the police barricade reached the Parliament building in New Baneswhar and began attacking, vandalizing, and setting fire to security personnel on duty and public property, an emergency District Security Committee meeting was held at the Command Post at 12:00 PM, issuing a curfew order to come into effect from 12:30 PM in the Baneswhar area, and directing Narasinghdal Gana at Singha Durbar, Bhadrakali, the District Police Office, Armed Police Force Nepal No. 20 Gana (Rescue and Disaster) at Sinamangal, and Armed Police Force Nepal No. 18 Gana at Nilbharhhari for implementation of that order. At that time, journalists from various media were repeatedly contacting me about the events. I had informed all media that the curfew decision had been made from 12:30 PM. As soon as the curfew decision was made, security personnel were directed to announce the curfew by microphone in the New Baneswhar area, and to make it more effective, the Armed Police Superintendent Jeevan K.C., who was at the Command Post, was also sent to Baneswhar by the District Chief. Additional assistance was requested from the Nepal Army since security personnel were insufficient to control the demonstrations. After the curfew was imposed, although it was expected that demonstrators would disperse, disregarding that, demonstrators blocked the roads among trucks carrying Nepal Army security personnel going from Singha Durbar toward Baneswhar to implement the curfew, and additional assistance could not reach the International Convention Center area.

Even after the curfew was imposed, demonstrators continued to vandalize around the Parliament building. Thereafter, the Chief District Officer directed that force be gradually applied in accordance with the principles of force use to protect the Parliament building, and in my capacity as spokesperson I informed the media of that content. I informed the media that the directive from the District Chief was not to fire metal bullets but to use rubber bullets if necessary.

The District Security Committee had given clear directives to security personnel to bring the situation under control using only minimum force, keeping in mind the participation of students and children as well. Although the number of demonstrators participating in protests organized by political parties can generally be estimated, the number of demonstrators who could participate in the demonstration called by the Gen Z generation on that day was far larger than what the state apparatus had estimated. Contrary to the assessment that the demonstration would occur in a civilized and decent manner, the sudden destructive activities made it very difficult for security personnel to bring the situation under control.

On 2082/5/24, with the intention of preventing further damage, a curfew order was issued for the Kathmandu Metropolitan area to come into effect from 8:30 AM until further notice  but since demonstrators did not comply with that order, the situation went out of control and very large damage occurred.

As vandalism incidents were occurring in various parts of the district and it appeared that security personnel were unable to bring the situation under control, at around 10:00 AM I, along with officials present at the District Administration Command Post, also went to the Ministry of Home Affairs. After approximately 15 minutes at the Ministry of Home Affairs, as directed by the Chief District Officer, everyone went to the Nepal Army's Nilbharahi Gana at Singha Durbar and stayed in that Gana throughout the rest of the day.
As the situation gradually normalized, I have been regularly re-engaged in office services from Bhadra 26 onward.
23. Prison Administrator Baburam Aryal, Prison Office Dillibazar:

I have been serving as office chief at the Prison Office Dillibazar since Baisakh 2082. On 2082/05/23 and 24, while the Gen Z generation was demonstrating in Kathmandu and various parts of the country demanding national interest, good governance, and corruption control, I was working at that same office. Prior to the movement, there was no estimate of the risk of the office being burned. No Early Warning regarding Unrest or Breakout Risk at the prison had come from security and intelligence agencies. Prior to the movement, the prison had CCTV, Watch Tower, and Parameter Fencing in good condition but no Alarm System. On Bhadra 23, 2082, the prison situation was normal. I was not even aware of the country-wide movement that occurred on the 24th and the attack that also took place at the prison. In connection with that, since the security threat at the prison where I was working also increased, I had written letters and repeatedly sent SMS messages to the Chief District Officer to send additional security personnel and made reports. I had also made reports to the Department of Prison Management about the ground situation at that time. I was continuously in discussion with the Nepal Police Commander  a Police Inspector  deployed for prison security. The situation was normal throughout the day. At approximately 17:00, around 400-500 demonstrators arrived in front of the prison's main gate and became active in demonstrations, slogan chanting, and vandalism; while security personnel were managing that demonstration, at approximately 18:00, demonstrators brought a bus (later learned to be belonging to the Commission for the Investigation of Abuse of Authority) and rammed the main gate with it to break in, then set fire to the same bus; thereafter they also set fire to the nearby police barracks, CCTV operation room, garage, and other facilities, causing complete damage to everything. Later, demonstrators fled due to aerial fire action by security personnel. On that day, there was no significant reaction from prisoners inside the prison. The following morning, from early Bhadra 25, prisoners began chanting various slogans targeting the jailer and security personnel, saying that prisoners from other prisons had been released outside and only they had not been released from our prison. They demanded that they too be released with a document. Despite repeated attempts to explain by police and our administration, prisoners did not comply, did not stay in their rooms, came outside, started slogan marches, and broke the channel gate inside the prison and began coming outside; police tried to control the situation with aerial fire. During that time, after I requested additional assistance from the Nepal Army, one unit of the army arrived at the prison. Prisoners attempted to break through 2 security gates and come out. They also set fire to where they lived. During that process, 20 prisoners escaped by climbing over the perimeter wall and through the main gate. Other prisoners were successfully kept under control. On that day, there were a total of 669 prisoners and detainees at the prison. Later, 457 prisoners and detainees remaining in the prison were transferred to the Central Prison Office at Jagannath Dewal on that same day; the remainder stayed in that prison. Of those who escaped, 9 have since returned to prison, and 11 remain at large. The security commander and I were in continuous contact on that day. There is no Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) for prison management but there is an operating procedure. Internal and external administration has been operating according to that procedure. On that day, as the movement occurred across the country and prisoners escaped from prisons and a situation was created where security could not be provided, prisoners escaped. During the movement, there was satisfactory cooperation from the internal administration including wardens and chief wardens. My recommendations for securing the prison are: increase security force establishment immediately; construct prison buildings that are physically strong and robust in the medium term; and construct prisons classified according to type of crime for the long term, keeping prisoners separated. Prisoners must be housed according to prison capacity.
24. Account of Acting Prison Administrator Baikuntha Raj Regmi, Central Prison Jagannath Dewal:

I am a branch officer on the establishment of the Central Prison Office Jagannath Dewal. On Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082, during the Gen Z movement, I was the acting prison administrator of the Prison Office Jagannath Dewal, Kathmandu. There was no estimate that demonstrators participating in the Gen Z movement would engage in activities such as attacking, vandalizing, and arson at the prison; no Early Warning regarding Unrest or Breakout Risk at the prison had come from security and intelligence agencies. The prison has a CCTV Watch System and Tower Parameter Fencing but no Alarm System. On Bhadra 24, 2082, it was heard that demonstrators were committing vandalism and arson at various government buildings including the Federal Parliament building; in connection with that, at around 19:30 in the evening, a large group of demonstrators arrived and began stone-pelting and attempting to vandalize all three gates of the detention facility  in the process, three windows of the prison office were broken and the main gate CCTV was vandalized. After that, prisoners broke all three doors of the detention facility, came outside, and advanced toward the main gate; Nepal Police on prison security duty at the main gate tried to stop them  pressure from demonstrators from outside and prisoners from inside meant police were also unable to stop them, resulting in prisoners successfully escaping. However, since the Nepal Army's National Service Corps office was adjacent to the prison, the movement of soldiers prevented arson and vandalism of vehicles at the prison and security side. The internal security situation of the prison was good; I asked everyone not to panic, not to engage in unruly activities such as vandalism and arson, and to stay peacefully inside the reform home, and also gave the assurance of working toward the services and facilities that detainees and prisoners are entitled to by law. Since vandalism and arson were occurring on all sides, there was no possibility of arranging additional security personnel. There is no Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) for prison management  there is a prison operation procedure. As acting prison administrator, responsibility for the escape of prisoners remained with me, but on that day I kept trying until the end to the best of my positional capacity and ability with the assistance of available security personnel  because of which, of the total 3,880 prisoners and detainees, I was successful in keeping 363 in prison. The remaining 3,517 escaped; of those who escaped, we have been successful in bringing back 2,317; the remaining 1,200 prisoners and detainees are still at large, and the search operation continues. Guards, AR, head wardens, and sub-wardens of the internal administration provided satisfactory cooperation. For securing the prison  security establishment should be increased immediately; structurally strong prisons should be built in the medium term; and in the long term, prisons must be built according to crime classification. Prisoners must be housed according to prison capacity.
5.1.3 Statement of the Nepal Army:
25. Statement of Chief of Army Staff General Ashok Raj Sigdel:

I joined the Nepal Army on Falgun 3, 2043, and have been serving as Chief of Army Staff since Bhadra 24, 2081. In my capacity as a member of the National Security Council, I have discharged my responsibilities in accordance with the spirit of the constitution and law during the complex and difficult circumstances that arose during the demonstrations by the Gen Z generation on Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082. At the Central Security Committee meeting held on Bhadra 22, information had been received from the National Investigation Department and other security agencies that the movement could be extensive. However, since a formal National Security Council meeting had not yet been held by that date, a special strategic discussion could not take place. On Bhadra 23, as the security situation in the Parliament building area became increasingly complex, I had directed that necessary security preparations be made in coordination with other security agencies as per previous decisions, and had put subordinate units on alert to be ready for curfew implementation upon the request of local administration. On that day, the Nepal Army was not deployed and security arrangements were implemented under the leadership of the Ministry of Home Affairs.

At approximately 20:00 on Bhadra 23, the 39th meeting of the National Security Council was held at the Prime Minister's residence, Baluwatar. The Nepal Army is not an institution that automatically takes to the streets  the constitutional arrangement is that it can only be deployed after constitutional and legal procedures are completed, through the recommendation of the Government of Nepal Council of Ministers and a decision by the President. By the time the meeting was held, the Home Minister had already resigned, the ban on closed social media had already been lifted, and it was assessed that the agitators' demands would be addressed  in that situation, no concrete estimate had been made that difficult circumstances would arise on the 24th, so there was no decision or discussion regarding army deployment.

In the morning of Bhadra 24, during discussions at the Prime Minister's residence, the Prime Minister asked me for my suggestions. I presented two options: first, since it was understood that some senior officials of his own political party were advising him to resign, resignation might be appropriate to go toward a political solution to prevent further human casualties; second, if constitutionally recommended, the Nepal Army was fully prepared to control the situation. However, after the Prime Minister resigned, the security situation became even more anarchic, and the Nepal Army progressively issued press releases and appeals, and from 22:00 that night was fully deployed and actively played a role in restoring peace and order.

I reject the allegation that there was a lack of coordination among security agencies. The heads of all three security agencies were in continuous contact and joint discussion, and even in dangerous situations, the Nepal Army protected and rescued members of other security agencies. The Nepal Army played a leading role in protecting Singha Durbar, the President's residence, the Prime Minister's residence, Tribhuvan International Airport, fuel depots, prisons, and other sensitive structures, where extreme restraint was exercised to minimize human casualties. It is necessary for everyone to be clear that the Nepal Army does not deploy without a clear mandate and constitutional provisions. The constitutional arrangement is that the army can only be deployed in accordance with Articles 266(1), 267(6), and 273(1) of Nepal's constitution.

Although complaints were heard that requests for the Nepal Army's assistance on Bhadra 24 were not addressed in time, that is not the reality. The Nepal Army used fire trucks to control the fire that had broken out at the National Data Center at Singha Durbar and the Ministry of Finance's Data Center. Furthermore, the main structures of the President's residence and Prime Minister's residence, Tribhuvan International Airport and airports throughout the country, fuel depots, prisons, and other Vital Installations were protected at the Nepal Army's initiative. Agitated crowds that had gathered outside the Singha Durbar and President's Office perimeter were sent away through verbal warnings, more than approximately 46 rounds of aerial fire, and by repeatedly physically pushing them away from the main gate and persuading them. Although the Nepal Army suggested imposing curfew from 05:00 AM on Bhadra 24, curfew in Kathmandu was only announced from 08:30 AM  by which time there was already a significant presence of demonstrators, so that curfew could not be effective.

I prioritized human security over physical structures. My view is that criticism regarding army deployment must be evaluated in the context of the then constitutional constraints and circumstances. Joint operations were conducted to recover looted weapons and bring back escaped prisoners, public appeals were made to return weapons, and the coordinated security mechanism was kept active.

Finally, my conclusion is that to prevent such events from recurring in the future, the state's administrative and security apparatus must be freed from political interference and strengthened and made effective on a legal basis, the National Security Council must be made more active and responsible, and deep coordination among security agencies and long-term structural reform are necessary.
26. Assistant General Manoj Baidwar, Security Commander of the President's Office, Shital Niwas:

I was assigned to the President's Office from 2081/04/18 and was at Shital Niwas on Bhadra 23. Although demonstrations by the Gen Z generation took place at various locations on that day, no abnormal security problem arose in the Shital Niwas area.

On Bhadra 24, at around 11:00 AM, information was received that demonstrators were gathering in various locations. Keeping in mind the possibility that these groups could target any sensitive location, troops were reorganized giving highest priority to the security of Shital Niwas, which was under my responsibility. Duties were deployed in blocking position on the southern side of Gate No. 2 of Shital Niwas and on the road near Maharajgunj Kanti Children's Hospital. The remaining troops were kept in post, standby, and stop-and-go positions around the Shital Niwas premises. Gates No. 1, No. 2, and the western gates were held as Strong Holds to strengthen the security perimeter. I was continuously verifying the security of Shital Niwas and the Honorable President through on-site inspection and radio set.

At around 12:30 PM, the Honorable President called me along with all advisors and discussed the overall situation. I presented information that the situation was still under control at that time and that the deployed troops were stopping demonstrators gathered on the street through persuasion. Since information regarding demonstrators' activities was continuously being received through various channels, alertness was maintained accordingly. At around 14:00, the Honorable President again called me to get information about the situation. He expressed his wish to remain safely at Shital Niwas until the last moment. I also clarified that Shital Niwas was safe until then and that preparations were ready to take him to an alternative safe location only in the final situation.

At around 15:00, after receiving information that the number of demonstrators was increasing significantly and that a large group was advancing toward Shital Niwas from the direction of the former Baluwatar, it became clear that Shital Niwas could be the potential target of all demonstrators. Keeping that situation in mind, since the first priority was the Honorable President's security, I presented the suggestion that he needed to be immediately moved to a safe location. With the assistance of the ADC team and the Special Security Directorate, at around 15:15 the Honorable President and family were successfully transported to a safe location by vehicle.

Even after this, I continued to be in continuous contact through radio set and mobile with the commander of troops deployed at Shital Niwas, military officers of the Presidential Secretariat, and the Special Security Directorate commander to understand the situation, and was continuously directing them to prevent damage to Shital Niwas through persuasion and use of minimum force as much as possible. At around 15:20, a large number of demonstrators attempted to enter through Gates No. 1 and No. 2. Although the troops stopped the entry attempt from Gate No. 2, a large number of demonstrators entered Shital Niwas through the low old wall on the right and left of Gate No. 1.

During that process, deployed troops continuously tried to persuade people not to enter, but since firing on the extremely agitated crowd could cause large human casualties, fire was not opened with weapons. The troops fell back somewhat and focused on protecting the southern office area and western residential area of Shital Niwas. The agitated crowd began vandalism and arson around the area, and at around 16:00 the fire became extensive. Nevertheless, since the troops were physically present until the last moment, continuing to persuade and preventing damage from spreading to other areas, we were successful in keeping all the southern office buildings, vehicles in the parking area, and the western President's residential building completely safe. Due to the old structure, carpets, curtains, and furniture, the fire spread quickly, causing damage to the eastern and northern parts of Shital Niwas and the prefab houses.

Inside Shital Niwas, only a small unit of 9 Nepal Police personnel was deployed for coordination purposes, and the Armed Police Force was not inside the inner premises. From the Nepal Army's side, 224 troops were deployed. Neither aerial fire nor tear gas was used against the agitated crowd of demonstrators, because in the situation where residential and commercial buildings were surrounding the area, there was a visible risk of causing serious human casualties.

In my understanding, the main reasons why partial damage occurred at Shital Niwas were: giving highest priority to ensuring no human casualties and using minimum force, and placing the security of the Honorable President and family in first priority in accordance with the Distinguished Person Security Procedure 2081. No weapons were lost. Throughout the entire chain of events, I coordinated with the Combat Operations Department following the chain of command and adhered to the security procedure as per received directives.

Finally, based on the experience of this incident  where it became evident that large damage can occur even from unarmed demonstrators in such situations  my conclusion is that police deployment must be gradual and planned, Nepal Police and Armed Police Force must play a primary role in accordance with the concept of Graduated Response with the Nepal Army deployed only as a last resort, and the walls, wire fencing, and multi-layered security structure of VVIP/VIP residential and office areas must be strengthened.
27. Statement of Lieutenant Colonel Diwakar Khadka, Security Commander, Prime Minister's Residence Baluwatar:
On Bhadra 23, I was present at the Prime Minister's residence and no abnormal problem arose in the security situation at that location on that day.

Until the morning of Bhadra 24, the situation in the Prime Minister's residence area was normal. However, reports were being received that uncontrolled demonstrations were occurring in outer areas. At around 14:00, information was received that demonstrators were advancing toward Baluwatar. Until approximately 14:30, Nepal Police and Armed Police Force deployed in the outer perimeter of the Prime Minister's residence were trying to stop the agitators, but information was received that the situation was becoming increasingly sensitive as the size and anger of the crowd grew.

Meanwhile, assessing the security risk through the chain of command, at 14:55 the decision to give highest priority to the Prime Minister's life and security and to rescue him to a safe location using Nepal Army aerial assets was implemented. This decision was made completely in accordance with SOP, security procedures, and in coordination with higher authorities.
